# Stripe Atlas vs Clerky vs Capbase: Which Incorporation Service Wins in 2026

Published: April 28, 2026 (traztech.ca)

## Summary

Head-to-head comparison of three major formation services, written by practitioners advising founders. Most valuable for the nuanced recommendation framework: Atlas for solo founders/small teams doing SaaS who will process payments through Stripe; Clerky for founders expecting heavy SAFE activity or unusual cofounder dynamics. The non-resident EIN section is especially important - Atlas has the most documented non-resident path.

## Key quotations / statistics

### Stripe Atlas
- "One-time $500 fee. Includes Delaware filing fees, registered agent for the first year, EIN application (with an expedited path for non-residents), bylaws, founder stock issuance, and a built-in tax election for 83(b) filing."
- "It ties directly into the Stripe payments stack, your processor account is provisioned during incorporation, not after."
- Year 1: $500. Year 2: registered agent renewal (~$100) + Delaware franchise tax ($400-$450 minimum). "Roughly $550/year ongoing. Cheapest if you don't need ongoing legal templates."

### Clerky
- "$425 for 'Clerky Library' formation, plus $99/year for ongoing access to legal templates. State filing fees (Delaware franchise tax, registered agent renewal) are extra."
- "Documents are drafted by Orrick lawyers (a top startup law firm) and the templates cover post-formation events that Atlas doesn't, option grants, advisor agreements, SAFE notes, priced rounds."
- "No EIN service for US founders included by default; non-residents pay extra. Clerky requires you to handle EIN yourself or pay a third party, meaning a 4 to 8 week wait via fax in most cases."
- Year 1: ~$700. Year 2: ~$650 (Library subscription + filing fees).
- "The Library subscription pays for itself the moment you issue a SAFE or option grant without involving a lawyer."

### Non-resident EIN: CRITICAL DISTINCTION
- "Atlas has the most documented non-resident path and a paid expedited option that gets the EIN in 7 to 14 days."
- "Clerky requires you to handle EIN yourself or pay a third party, meaning a 4 to 8 week wait via fax in most cases."
- "For a non-resident founder who needs to be operational in under 30 days, Atlas is almost always the right call."

### Recommendation framework from practitioners:
- "For most pre-seed SaaS founders: Atlas plus a free Carta or Pulley account for cap table tracking. Cheapest, fastest, lowest-risk path."
- "We use Clerky when a founder has unusual cofounder dynamics or expects heavy SAFE activity in year one."
- "None of these three services will mess up your incorporation. All three produce VC-fundable Delaware C-corps."
- "Atlas is the choice for solo founders or two-person teams building SaaS, processing payments through Stripe, cheapest/fastest path."
- "Clerky is the choice for founders expecting 5+ SAFEs in first year, complex equity (reverse vesting on IP, advisor grants), or who want lawyer-grade templates for 18 months without hourly attorney rates. Used by most YC companies."

## Annotations for stinger-forge

- **guides/01-formation-platforms.md:** This is the most current (April 2026) and practitioner-grounded comparison. Use the recommendation framework as the basis for the decision table. Note Clerky's updated pricing ($425 + $99/year) vs. Atlas ($500 flat).
- **guides/02-ein-workflow.md:** The non-resident EIN problem is a critical branching point. Atlas > Clerky > Doola for non-resident speed.
- **guides/06-attorney-triggers.md:** "Unusual cofounder dynamics, complex IP (reverse vesting on existing IP, advisor grants)" = use Clerky or get an attorney.
- IMPORTANT CORRECTION: Clerky pricing in this 2026 source ($425 + $99/year Library) differs from older sources quoting $799/$819. stinger-forge should note this and verify directly.
